summary: American philosopher, logician, and 'father of pragmatism.' Author of 'How to Make Our Ideas Clear' and 'The Fixation of Belief,' published in Popular Science Monthly. Developed triadic semiotics (icon, index, symbol), the categories of firstness/secondness/thirdness, abductive reasoning, synechism, and tychism.

works.name: The Fixation of Belief
works.description: An 1877 essay published in Popular Science Monthly as part of Peirce's 'Illustrations of the Logic of Science' series. It outlines four methods for settling opinion: tenacity (clinging spasmodically to existing views), authority (institutions designed to keep correct doctrines), the a priori method, and the scientific method, which Peirce advocates as the best. The method of tenacity is compared to an ostrich burying its head in the sand.

clues.clue: four methods for settling opinion: tenacity, authority, a priori, scientific method
examples: argued that the best of four methods for settling opinion was the scientific method
he rejects the methods of tenacity, authority and a priori reasoning, favoring science

clues.clue: method of tenacity — clinging to existing views; compared to ostrich burying head in sand
examples: discusses men who 'cling spasmodically to the views they already take'
it compares the method of tenacity with an ostrich who has buried his head in the sand

clues.clue: method of authority — institutions designed to 'keep correct doctrines' and 'prevent contrary doctrines'
examples: institutions designed to 'keep correct doctrines' and 'prevent contrary doctrines'

clues.clue: published in Popular Science Monthly as part of 'Illustrations of the Logic of Science'
examples: advocated for the scientific method above the 'method of tenacity' in one of several 1870s essays for Popular Science Monthly

works.name: How to Make Our Ideas Clear
works.description: An 1878 essay and early statement of pragmatism. It proposes three grades of clearness of apprehension and formulates the pragmatic maxim: that the meaning of a concept is determined by its practical effects. The essay discusses the parallelogram of forces example and contrasts distinct and confused conceptions. It defines truth as the opinion 'fated to be ultimately agreed to by all who investigate.'

clues.clue: three grades of clearness of apprehension; pragmatic maxim
examples: an essay that discusses the parallelogram law
categorized grades of clearness in 'How to Make Our Ideas Clear'

clues.clue: truth is the opinion 'fated to be ultimately agreed to by all who investigate'
examples: he defined the real as the object represented in the truth, which is the opinion 'agreed to by all who investigate'

clues.clue: parallelogram of forces / parallelogram law example
examples: another work discusses the example of adding accelerations together using a parallelogram

clues.clue: nickel vs. five coppers example — doubt and belief
examples: illustrated the concepts of doubt and belief by asking whether one should pay for a horse-car with a nickel or with five coppers

works.name: Semiotics & On a New List of Categories
works.description: Peirce's triadic theory of signs divides them into icons, indexes, and symbols. Icons resemble their objects, indexes evidence a real connection (smoke for fire), and symbols are arbitrary. His 'On a New List of Categories' (1867) establishes the central categories of firstness (quality/feeling), secondness (relation/reaction), and thirdness (representation/habit). These map to his 66 classes of signs developed with Victoria, Lady Welby. Peirce also introduced the type-token distinction.

clues.clue: triadic semiotics: icon, index, symbol
examples: every sign is either an icon, index, or symbol in this thinker's theory of semiotics
icons, indices, and symbols were three examples of 'hypostatic abstraction'

clues.clue: firstness, secondness, thirdness — quality, relation, representation
examples: listed 'quality,' 'relation,' and 'representation' as 'firstness,' 'secondness,' and 'thirdness'
he defined firstness, secondness, and thirdness, as respectively ideas, brute facts, and habits

clues.clue: index: smoke is an index of fire; evidences real connection
examples: this third type of sign represents an object by virtue of real connection to it. Smoke is this type of sign in relation to fire

clues.clue: interpretant — the effect of a sign on the person processing it
examples: the connect of a sign or 'representamen' to an object produces this, which is the effect of the sign on the person processing it

clues.clue: type-token distinction — first made in an article for The Monist

clues.clue: 66 classes of signs based on 'dynamic object' with Victoria, Lady Welby
examples: a member of this movement worked out 66 classes based on concepts like the 'dynamic object' in collaboration with Victoria, Lady Welby

works.name: Synechism, Tychism & Evolutionary Love
works.description: Peirce developed theories of continuity (synechism) and absolute chance (tychism) in a series of essays for The Monist. Synechism holds that all thought and consciousness must be fully continuous. Tychism argues that properties of the universe evolved through 'absolute chance.' 'The Law of Mind' discusses the human tendency to view the universe as continuous and how ideas tend to spread, gaining generality and losing intensity. 'Evolutionary Love' (agapeism) argues against social Darwinism.

clues.clue: synechism (continuity) and tychism (absolute chance)
examples: developed theories regarding the importance of continuity and 'absolute chance,' respectively called synechism and tychism

clues.clue: The Law of Mind — ideas naturally tend to spread, gaining generality and losing intensity
examples: this philosopher claimed that ideas naturally tend to spread, gaining generality and losing intensity, in a notion that he called 'The Law of Mind'

clues.clue: Evolutionary Love / agapeism — argued against social Darwinism
examples: this thinker argued against social Darwinism by claiming that evolution involves what they call its 'agapeism'

clues.clue: series of six essays in The Monist arguing properties of the universe evolved in Darwinian fashion
examples: wrote a series of six essays in The Monist arguing that properties of the universe had evolved in Darwinian fashion

works.name: Logic & Mathematics
works.description: Peirce was a major logician who introduced abductive reasoning (inference to the best explanation), showed NAND and NOR were Sheffer functions decades before Sheffer, developed three-valued logic (V, L, F), and proposed Peirce's law (if 'P then Q' implies P, then P must be true). He expanded on De Morgan's work in logic, coined the term 'likelihood functions,' and used the 'ampliative' for arguments whose conclusions go beyond premises. He introduced phaneroscopy (phenomenology) and attacked Cartesian methods of doubt.

clues.clue: abductive reasoning — third type of inference alongside deduction and induction
examples: this philosopher divided inference into the categories of abduction, deduction, and induction

clues.clue: Peirce's law — if 'P then Q' implies P, then P must be true
examples: a law named for this thinker states that P must be true if there is a proposition Q such that 'P then Q' implies P

clues.clue: showed NAND and NOR were Sheffer functions decades before Sheffer

clues.clue: three-valued logic — V (true), L (indeterminate), F (false)
examples: this American philosopher's system of three-valued logic uses the symbols V, L, and F

clues.clue: expanded De Morgan's work in logic; coined 'abductive reasoning' and 'rheme'
examples: De Morgan's work in logic was greatly expanded by this other philosopher

clues.clue: attacked Cartesian methods of doubt as a 'salad' not built on sincere foundations
examples: attacked the methods of inquiry used by this philosopher as a 'salad'

clues.clue: phaneroscopy — coined to replace 'phenomenology'; the phaneron is the world as interpreted by senses
examples: thinking the word 'phenomenology' had become too loaded, this thinker coined the term 'phaneroscopy'

works.name: General / Biographical
works.description: Peirce is identified as the 'father of pragmatism' and an American philosopher. He renamed his philosophy 'pragmaticism' after breaking with William James. He published extensively in Popular Science Monthly and The Monist. He was banned from Harvard, taught at Johns Hopkins under Daniel Coit Gilman, and his bad temper and serial adultery ruined his academic prospects. His father was a mathematician who claimed mathematics is the 'science that draws necessary conclusions.' Umberto Eco focused on Peirce's reading of Kant in Kant and the Platypus.

clues.clue: 'father of pragmatism'; renamed philosophy 'pragmaticism' after breaking with James

this man began referring to his philosophy as 'pragmaticism,' rather than pragmatism

clues.clue: Umberto Eco focused on Peirce's reading of Kant in Kant and the Platypus
examples: in Chapter 2 of Kant and the Platypus, Umberto Eco focuses on this philosopher's reading of Kant

comprehensive_summary:
Charles Sanders Peirce is the American philosopher known as the 'father of pragmatism.' His foundational essays 'How to Make Our Ideas Clear' (1878) and 'The Fixation of Belief' (1877), published in Popular Science Monthly, establish the pragmatic maxim and outline four methods for settling opinion: tenacity, authority, the a priori method, and the scientific method, which Peirce advocates as best.

Peirce's triadic semiotics divides signs into icons, indexes, and symbols. His 'On a New List of Categories' (1867) introduces the central concepts of firstness (quality/feeling), secondness (relation/reaction), and thirdness (representation/habit). He developed 66 classes of signs with Victoria, Lady Welby, and introduced the type-token distinction.

In logic and mathematics, Peirce introduced abductive reasoning (a third type of inference alongside deduction and induction), demonstrated that NAND and NOR were Sheffer functions decades before Sheffer, developed three-valued logic, and formulated Peirce's law. He expanded on De Morgan's work, coined 'phaneroscopy' to replace 'phenomenology,' and attacked Cartesian methods of doubt.

Peirce developed theories of continuity (synechism) and absolute chance (tychism) in essays for The Monist. 'The Law of Mind' discusses how ideas naturally spread, and 'Evolutionary Love' introduces agapeism as an alternative to social Darwinism. He also wrote 'A Neglected Argument for the Reality of God.' He renamed his philosophy 'pragmaticism' after breaking with William James. His academic career was ruined by his bad temper and serial adultery, leading to his ban from Harvard, though he taught at Johns Hopkins under Daniel Coit Gilman.
